attributes.stem: A 45-year-old woman is undergoing an elective laparoscopic cholecystectomy under general anaesthesia. She has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. The surgery has been proceeding for 35 minutes. Her pulse rate is 75/min, blood pressure 120/80 mmHg, SpO2 99% on an FiO2 of 0.3, and temperature 36.8°C. The end-tidal carbon dioxide has gradually risen from 35 to 45 mmHg over the past 20 minutes despite no changes to ventilator settings.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely cause of the rising end-tidal carbon dioxide?
attributes.explanation:
This patient demonstrates a gradual rise in end-tidal CO2 during laparoscopic surgery with otherwise stable vital signs. Carbon dioxide absorption from insufflation is the most likely cause, as CO2 is commonly used for pneumoperitoneum during laparoscopic procedures. The gradual rise over 20 minutes is typical of this phenomenon.

Increased metabolic rate alone would be unlikely to cause such a significant rise in end-tidal CO2 over this short timeframe. Additionally, one would expect to see associated changes in other parameters such as heart rate if metabolic rate was significantly increased.

Malignant hyperthermia typically presents with a rapid and dramatic rise in end-tidal CO2 along with other features including tachycardia, muscle rigidity, and hyperthermia. The gradual rise and absence of other concerning features makes this diagnosis unlikely.

Hypoventilation would be immediately apparent through changes in minute ventilation on the anaesthetic machine and would typically be accompanied by changes in other parameters such as tidal volume or respiratory rate.

Misplaced endotracheal tube would typically present with acute changes in ventilation parameters, difficulty ventilating, or changes in airway pressures. A gradual rise in end-tidal CO2 would be unusual with tube displacement, and you would expect to see associated hypoxia.

The management of CO2 absorption during laparoscopy typically involves increasing minute ventilation to compensate for the additional CO2 load. This can be achieved by increasing respiratory rate or tidal volume. The rise in end-tidal CO2 is usually self-limiting and resolves after desufflation of the pneumoperitoneum.
learningPoints: Carbon dioxide absorption from peritoneal insufflation during laparoscopic surgery causes a gradual rise in end-tidal CO2.
A steady rise in end-tidal CO2 without other concerning features suggests CO2 absorption rather than other causes.
Malignant hyperthermia presents with a rapid rise in end-tidal CO2 along with other features including tachycardia and muscle rigidity.
references: C.N. Gutt, T. Oniu, A. Mehrabi, P. Schemmer, A. Kashfi, T. Kraus, M.W. Büchler; Circulatory and Respiratory Complications of Carbon Dioxide Insufflation. Dig Surg 1 March 2004; 21 (2): 95–105. https://doi.org/10.1159/000077038

Farman et al,. Effect of insufflated CO2 during laparoscopic surgeries on end tidal CO2 concentration using capnography. Biomedical Letters, 2022. Available from: https://api.semanticscholar.org/CorpusID:247770327.
